concept.title: XML Namespaces
concept.shortdesc: Namespaces prevent naming conflicts when XML vocabularies from different sources are combined in a single document by qualifying element and attribute names with a unique URI.

section.title: Why do we need namespaces?
section.p: Imagine two XML vocabularies that both define a <title> element — one for books and one for job titles. When these vocabularies appear in the same document, a parser cannot tell which <title> is which. Namespaces solve this by giving each vocabulary a unique identifier.
section.codeblock: <!-- Without namespaces: ambiguous --> <record> <title>Learning XML</title> <!-- Book title? --> <title>Senior Engineer</title> <!-- Job title? --> </record>

section.title: Declaring namespaces
section.p: A namespace is declared using the xmlns attribute. There are two forms:
Default namespace — applies to the element and all descendants without a prefix:
section.codeblock: <bookstore xmlns="http://example.com/books"> <title>Learning XML</title> <!-- in the books namespace --> </bookstore>
section.p: Prefixed namespace — uses a short prefix to identify elements from a specific vocabulary:
section.codeblock: <record xmlns:bk="http://example.com/books" xmlns:hr="http://example.com/hr"> <bk:title>Learning XML</bk:title> <!-- Book title --> <hr:title>Senior Engineer</hr:title> <!-- Job title --> </record>
section.note: The namespace URI is just an identifier — it does not need to point to an actual web page. By convention, organizations use their domain name to ensure uniqueness.

section.title: Namespace scope
section.p: A namespace declaration is in scope for the element where it is declared and all of that element's descendants. You can override a namespace on a child element:
section.codeblock: <root xmlns="http://example.com/default"> <child>In default namespace</child> <other xmlns="http://example.com/other"> <child>In other namespace</child> </other> </root>

section.title: Namespaces in DITA
section.p: DITA topics typically do not use namespace prefixes because DITA has its own vocabulary that does not collide with other schemas. However, you will encounter namespaces when:

ul.li: Writing XSLT stylesheets that transform DITA content
Working with XSD-based DITA grammar files (DITA 1.3+ uses RELAX NG and XSD grammars)
Embedding foreign content (such as MathML or SVG) within DITA topics
